scenarios.reason: A 12.3 M☉ MS star would have L ≈ 6550 L☉ (Teff ≈ 24,000 K), dominating the SED in all optical/IR bands. No secondary component detected. SED is consistent with single evolved star.
M2 = 12.3 M☉ exceeds the Chandrasekhar limit (1.44 M☉) by 9×.
M2 = 12.3 M☉ exceeds the TOV limit (2.3 M☉) by 5×.

Two 6.2 M☉ MS stars: combined L ≈ 1158 L☉ vs. primary L ≈ 22 L☉. Would dominate SED. Stability requires P_inner < 42 d. No composite spectrum detected. However, a triple with two compact objects (e.g. NS+NS) cannot be excluded by photometry alone.

A 12.3 M☉ stripped He star would have Teff > 50,000 K and L > 10,000 L☉. Expected strong UV excess: predicted NUV ≈ 9 vs observed 17.5 (>2000× fainter). Not favoured but requires UV spectroscopy for firm exclusion.

Solution type is Orbital (astrometry only, no independent spectroscopic confirmation). Recent RV follow-up studies show many Gaia DR3 Orbital BH candidates fail spectroscopic checks. NSS significance (75.4σ) and RUWE (9.31) are supportive but not sufficient to exclude scan-angle biases or marginally resolved companion effects. Requires independent multi-epoch RV orbit.

At G = 11.25, source confusion is low but at b = 9.3° blending is not negligible. A coherent 424-d Keplerian signal at 75σ argues against pure noise, but the absence of independent RV confirmation means this cannot be fully excluded. Requires spectroscopic follow-up.
